skill.name: commit
skill.description: Helps draft Conventional Commit messages and guide safe, atomic git commit workflows.
skill.summary: Drafts clear Conventional Commit messages from staged changes.
skill.author: 0xmsc

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported weak cryptography and Ruby backtick execution, but the cited evidence is Markdown text, commit message placeholders, and inline examples. No malware, network access, secret access, prompt injection, or executable script behavior was found; the remaining risk is normal git command guidance that can modify repository state.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The high severity weak cryptography alerts point to commit message placeholders and prose such as description fields, not cryptographic code or algorithm use. These findings are false positives and do not indicate malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain Markdown text and commit message examples only. I found no cryptographic functions, imports, keys, hashes, or algorithm configuration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick alerts point to Markdown code fences and inline code examples. The files are documentation and do not contain executable Ruby, shell scripts, or dynamic command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All cited backticks are Markdown formatting around examples or command names. There is no interpreter context where those strings execute.

low_findings.title: Git Command Guidance Can Change Repository State
low_findings.description: The skill recommends git commands including add and commit. This is legitimate for the stated purpose, but users should expect repository state changes and confirm before committing or pushing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command guidance is explicit and relevant to a commit helper. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the skill also limits force pushing, amending, and interactive commands.

content.value_statement: Commit messages are often inconsistent or too broad for useful history. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code draft atomic Conventional Commit messages from staged work.
actual_capabilities: Reviews staged git changes before drafting a commit message.
Formats messages with Conventional Commit type, optional scope, subject, body, and footer.
Encourages one logical change per commit.
Suggests safe git status, diff, show, log, add, and commit usage.
Applies subject length and body wrapping guidance.
Uses a simple commit template for consistent structure.
limitations: It does not perform interactive git workflows such as patch add or interactive rebase.
It should not force push or amend commits without explicit user guidance.
It depends on the quality of staged changes and repository context.
It does not replace project-specific commit or release policies.

use_cases.title: Prepare a feature commit
use_cases.description: Draft a concise feature commit message after reviewing staged implementation changes.

use_cases.title: Clean up pull request history
use_cases.description: Split unrelated changes into clearer atomic commits before opening a review.

use_cases.title: Standardize team commit style
use_cases.description: Apply Conventional Commit structure across routine fixes, documentation, tests, and chores.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a basic commit message
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request after staging one small change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my staged changes and suggest one Conventional Commit message. Keep the subject under 50 characters.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the correct commit type
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs help selecting feat, fix, docs, test, refactor, or chore.
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the staged diff, identify the best Conventional Commit type, and explain the choice briefly before giving the final message.

prompt_templates.title: Create an atomic commit plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Several unrelated changes may be staged together.
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether my staged changes represent one logical commit. If not, propose an atomic commit split and message for each part.

prompt_templates.title: Draft release-aware commit text
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced change may need a body or breaking change footer.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the staged changes for release impact. Draft a Conventional Commit with a body and footer only if they add useful context.

output_examples.input: Staged changes add password reset email support and related tests.
output_examples.output: Suggested message: feat(auth): add password reset emails. Include a short body if the implementation changes user-facing recovery behavior.

output_examples.input: Staged changes correct typos in the README and update no source files.
output_examples.output: Suggested message: docs: correct README typos. No body is needed because the change is small and self-explanatory.

output_examples.input: Staged changes rename a public configuration option.
output_examples.output: Suggested message: refactor(config): rename legacy option. Add a breaking change footer if existing users must update configuration.

best_practices: Stage only one logical change before asking for a commit message.
Review the final message against project conventions before committing.
Use a body when it explains motivation, tradeoffs, or migration impact.
anti_patterns: Do not combine unrelated fixes, features, and formatting changes in one commit.
Do not use vague subjects such as update files or fix stuff.
Do not force push or amend shared history without clear user approval.

faq.question: Does this skill create commits automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide git commit commands, but users should confirm before any commit or push is made.

faq.question: Which commit format does it use?
faq.answer: It follows Conventional Commits with a type, optional scope, concise subject, optional body, and optional footer.

faq.question: Can it help split changes into atomic commits?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review staged changes and suggest whether they should be split into smaller logical commits.

faq.question: Does it support breaking change footers?
faq.answer: Yes. It can suggest a breaking change footer when staged changes indicate release impact.

faq.question: Can it run interactive git commands?
faq.answer: The skill states that interactive commands such as patch add and interactive rebase are outside its scope.

faq.question: Is this only for Claude?
faq.answer: No. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
